does: This paper addresses privacy leakage in graph federated learning when users withdraw by applying machine unlearning techniques to remove user information from trained models. The authors identify that standard unlearning methods degrade performance significantly in the GFL setting due to local message passing and global model collaboration. They propose two key mitigation strategies: orthogonal gradient steering that ensures unlearning updates minimally affect overall model performance by constraining them to be orthogonal to gradients from retained data, and the introduction of virtual clients maintained by the server to compensate for the loss of local graph structure information. The approach balances privacy preservation with model utility, enabling users to truly exit federated learning systems while maintaining reasonable model accuracy.
connects: This work bridges machine unlearning and federated learning literature, extending recent efforts in privacy-preserving decentralized learning. It builds on classical machine unlearning methods that aim to remove data influence from trained models, while addressing their known limitation of performance degradation. The paper connects to prior federated learning work that keeps data local but recognizes the persistent privacy risks identified in graph neural networks where local message passing can leak user information globally. It responds to regulatory pressures from GDPR and CCPA requiring user withdrawal rights, and extends foundational federated learning theory to handle the specific challenges of graph-structured data with complex interdependencies. The orthogonal gradient approach draws on optimization techniques from continual learning and privacy-preserving machine learning.
enables: This work opens directions for truly privacy-respecting federated learning systems where user withdrawal is both technically feasible and maintains model utility. It enables practical deployment of graph federated learning in regulated domains like healthcare and social networks where users have legal rights to data deletion. Future work could extend unlearning to handle more complex graph topologies, develop theoretical guarantees on privacy and utility tradeoffs, and explore efficient unlearning for dynamic graphs where users frequently join and leave. The virtual client mechanism could inspire new approaches to compensating for missing subgraph information in collaborative learning settings. This foundation supports development of certifiable unlearning procedures in federated contexts and enables investigation of adversarial unlearning scenarios where malicious clients attempt to extract information about withdrawn users.
